section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A pharmacist receives a prescription for 250 mL of potassium permanganate solution 1 in 8000 to be used as a soak for a patient with weeping eczema. How many milligrams of potassium permanganate are required to prepare this solution?
explanation: A 1 in 8000 solution contains 1 g in 8000 mL. Amount required = (1 g ÷ 8000 mL) × 250 mL = 0.03125 g = 31.25 mg.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 62-year-old man is prescribed chloramphenicol 0.5% w/v eye drops. The pharmacy compounds a specials order for 10 mL of solution. How many milligrams of chloramphenicol are required?
explanation: 0.5% w/v contains 0.5 g per 100 mL. Amount required = (0.5 g ÷ 100 mL) × 10 mL = 0.05 g = 50 mg.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: An IV infusion bag contains potassium chloride 40 mmol/L in sodium chloride 0.9% in a 1 litre bag. The relative molecular mass of potassium chloride is 74.5. How many grams of potassium chloride are contained in the full 1 litre bag? Give your answer to two decimal places.
explanation: Moles of KCl = 40 mmol = 0.04 mol. Mass = 0.04 mol × 74.5 g/mol = 2.98 g.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 34-year-old woman experiences anaphylaxis after a wasp sting in the pharmacy. She requires 500 micrograms of adrenaline by intramuscular injection. The pharmacy stocks adrenaline 1 in 1000 ampoules. What volume, in mL, of adrenaline 1 in 1000 should be administered?
explanation: Adrenaline 1 in 1000 contains 1 g in 1000 mL = 1 mg/mL = 1000 micrograms/mL. Volume = 500 micrograms ÷ 1000 micrograms/mL = 0.5 mL.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A neonatal unit receives a syringe labelled morphine sulfate 0.05% w/v. How many micrograms of morphine sulfate are contained in each 1 mL of this solution?
explanation: 0.05% w/v means 0.05 g per 100 mL = 0.0005 g/mL = 0.5 mg/mL = 500 micrograms/mL.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A pharmacist is compounding 100 g of a topical cream containing betamethasone valerate 0.1% w/w with menthol 1% w/w in aqueous cream. How many milligrams of betamethasone valerate are required?
explanation: 0.1% w/w contains 0.1 g per 100 g. Amount = (0.1 g ÷ 100 g) × 100 g = 0.1 g = 100 mg.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A pharmacist receives a query from a nurse about a multidose vial labelled lidocaine hydrochloride 20 mg/mL. What is this concentration expressed as a percentage strength (% w/v)?
explanation: 20 mg/mL = 20 mg per 1 mL = 2000 mg per 100 mL = 2 g per 100 mL = 2% w/v.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 1 litre bag of sodium chloride 0.9% w/v is to be administered to a patient with hyponatraemia. The relative molecular mass of sodium chloride is 58.5. How many millimoles of sodium are contained in the bag? Give your answer to the nearest whole number.
explanation: Mass NaCl in bag = 0.9 g/100 mL × 1000 mL = 9 g. Moles = 9 ÷ 58.5 = 0.1538 mol = 153.8 mmol. Each mole of NaCl yields 1 mole of Na⁺ ions, so 154 mmol of sodium (to nearest whole number).

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A water fluoridation scheme adds fluoride to drinking water at a concentration of 5 parts per million (ppm). How many milligrams of fluoride are contained in a 250 mL glass of this water? Give your answer to two decimal places.
explanation: 5 ppm in water ≈ 5 mg/L (since 1 L water ≈ 1 kg). Amount in 250 mL = 5 mg/L × 0.25 L = 1.25 mg.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A pharmacy sells 500 mL bottles of hand sanitiser containing ethanol 70% v/v. How many millilitres of pure ethanol are contained in each bottle?
explanation: 70% v/v contains 70 mL of ethanol per 100 mL of solution. Volume of ethanol = (70 mL ÷ 100 mL) × 500 mL = 350 mL.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 76-year-old patient with a pulmonary embolism is prescribed unfractionated heparin as a loading dose. The pharmacy supplies heparin sodium 5000 units/mL in 5 mL ampoules, and 5 mL is drawn up for the loading dose. How many units of heparin are administered?
explanation: Concentration = 5000 units/mL. Dose administered = 5000 units/mL × 5 mL = 25,000 units.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A pharmacist compounds 200 mL of hydrogen peroxide 1 in 200 mouthwash from a stock solution of hydrogen peroxide 6% w/v. How many millilitres of the 6% w/v stock solution are required? Give your answer to two decimal places.
explanation: 1 in 200 = 1 g in 200 mL = 0.5% w/v. Use C₁V₁ = C₂V₂: 6 × V₁ = 0.5 × 200, so V₁ = 100 ÷ 6 = 16.67 mL.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 67-year-old man is taking effervescent paracetamol 500 mg tablets, two tablets four times a day. Each tablet contains 388 mg of sodium. The recommended daily allowance (RDA) of sodium for an adult is 2 g per day. What percentage of this patient's RDA of sodium is contained in the total daily dose of effervescent paracetamol? Give your answer to the nearest whole number.
explanation: Daily sodium intake from medicine = 388 mg × 2 tablets × 4 doses = 3104 mg. As a % of RDA = (3104 ÷ 2000) × 100 = 155.2% ≈ 155%. (This illustrates why effervescent preparations are problematic for patients with hypertension or heart failure.)

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A pharmacist is asked to interpret a prescription for an infusion of glucose 1 in 4 w/v. What is this concentration expressed as a percentage strength?
explanation: 1 in 4 w/v means 1 g in 4 mL = 25 g in 100 mL = 25% w/v.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 45-year-old woman is prescribed co-amoxiclav 500/125 tablets, one to be taken three times a day for 7 days for cellulitis. Each tablet contains amoxicillin 500 mg and clavulanic acid 125 mg. What is the total quantity, in grams, of clavulanic acid she will receive over the full course?
explanation: Clavulanic acid per tablet = 125 mg. Tablets per course = 3 × 7 = 21. Total clavulanic acid = 125 mg × 21 = 2625 mg = 2.625 g.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A hospital aseptic unit reconstitutes a 250 mg vial of vancomycin in 50 mL of sodium chloride 0.9% to prepare an infusion. Assuming negligible displacement volume, what is the final concentration of vancomycin expressed as a percentage strength?
explanation: Concentration = 250 mg ÷ 50 mL = 5 mg/mL = 500 mg per 100 mL = 0.5 g per 100 mL = 0.5% w/v.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A pharmacist dilutes a stock solution by mixing 1 part of the original solution with 19 parts of water. The original solution was a 100% w/v concentrate. What is the final concentration of the diluted solution?
explanation: 1 part drug + 19 parts water = 20 parts total. Final concentration = (1 ÷ 20) × 100% = 5% w/v.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 58-year-old man with type 1 diabetes is prescribed insulin glargine 36 units subcutaneously at night. He has run out of his pre-filled pens and needs to use a 10 mL vial of insulin glargine 100 units/mL with a syringe. What volume, in mL, of insulin should he draw up? Give your answer to two decimal places.
explanation: Concentration = 100 units/mL. Volume = 36 units ÷ 100 units/mL = 0.36 mL. (Note: in practice, insulin should always be measured using an insulin-specific syringe to prevent fatal dosing errors.)

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 4-year-old boy weighing 18 kg requires a single loading dose of phenytoin 5 mg/kg PO for status epilepticus prophylaxis. Phenytoin oral suspension is available as 30 mg/5 mL. How many millilitres of the suspension should be administered?
explanation: Dose required = 5 mg/kg × 18 kg = 90 mg. Concentration of suspension = 30 mg per 5 mL = 6 mg/mL. Volume = 90 mg ÷ 6 mg/mL = 15 mL.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A pharmacist prepares 200 mL of calamine lotion BP, which contains zinc oxide 15% w/v. How many grams of zinc oxide are required to make this preparation?
explanation: 15% w/v = 15 g per 100 mL. Amount required = (15 g ÷ 100 mL) × 200 mL = 30 g.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A hospital pharmacy holds a stock solution of chlorhexidine gluconate 20% w/v. A ward requests 500 mL of chlorhexidine gluconate 0.05% w/v for skin antisepsis. How many millilitres of the 20% w/v stock solution are required to prepare the diluted solution?
explanation: Use C₁V₁ = C₂V₂. 20% × V₁ = 0.05% × 500 mL. V₁ = (0.05 × 500) ÷ 20 = 1.25 mL of stock, made up to 500 mL with diluent.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A laboratory technician performs three sequential 1 in 10 dilutions starting with a stock solution of 100 mg/mL. What is the final concentration after the third dilution?
explanation: Each 1 in 10 dilution divides the concentration by 10. After 3 dilutions: 100 ÷ 10 ÷ 10 ÷ 10 = 0.1 mg/mL.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A pharmacist prepares 250 mL of povidone-iodine 2% w/v solution from a 10% w/v stock. How many millilitres of water are required to dilute the stock to the final volume?
explanation: Volume of stock needed: C₁V₁ = C₂V₂ → 10 × V₁ = 2 × 250 → V₁ = 50 mL. Volume of water (diluent) = 250 − 50 = 200 mL.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A pharmacist adds 400 mL of water to 100 mL of a 5% w/v solution. What is the final concentration of the resulting solution?
explanation: Final volume = 100 + 400 = 500 mL. Mass of drug unchanged: (5 g/100 mL) × 100 mL = 5 g. New concentration = 5 g ÷ 500 mL = 1 g per 100 mL = 1% w/v.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A pharmacist is asked to prepare 100 mL of potassium permanganate 1 in 5000 from a stock solution of potassium permanganate 1 in 200. How many millilitres of the stock solution are required?
explanation: Convert ratios to %: 1 in 200 = 0.5% w/v; 1 in 5000 = 0.02% w/v. Use C₁V₁ = C₂V₂: 0.5 × V₁ = 0.02 × 100, so V₁ = 2 ÷ 0.5 = 4 mL.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A pharmacist needs to prepare 400 mL of a 2% w/v solution by mixing two stock solutions: a 5% w/v solution and a 1% w/v solution. Using alligation, how many millilitres of the 5% w/v stock solution are required?
explanation: By alligation, parts of 5% needed = (2 − 1) = 1 part; parts of 1% needed = (5 − 2) = 3 parts. Total = 4 parts = 400 mL, so 1 part = 100 mL. Volume of 5% stock = 100 mL.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A community pharmacy is preparing 500 mL of ethanol 70% v/v hand sanitiser from ethanol 96% v/v stock. How many millilitres of the 96% v/v ethanol are required? Give your answer to two decimal places.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A pharmacist is asked to prepare 60 g of hydrocortisone 0.5% w/w cream by diluting hydrocortisone 2.5% w/w cream with white soft paraffin (assumed inert). How many grams of the 2.5% w/w cream are required?
explanation: Use C₁W₁ = C₂W₂ (mass-based for w/w): 2.5 × W₁ = 0.5 × 60. W₁ = 30 ÷ 2.5 = 12 g of cream, with 48 g of white soft paraffin to make 60 g total.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A pharmacy compounds 500 mL of a chlorhexidine gluconate 0.2% w/v mouthwash for a hospital ward. The pharmacy uses chlorhexidine gluconate 5% w/v concentrate as the source. How many millilitres of the 5% concentrate are required?
explanation: C₁V₁ = C₂V₂: 5 × V₁ = 0.2 × 500. V₁ = 100 ÷ 5 = 20 mL. Made up to 500 mL with appropriate diluent.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A pharmacist has 200 mL of a 4% w/v drug solution and wants to dilute it to a final concentration of 1% w/v. How many millilitres of water must be added?
explanation: C₁V₁ = C₂V₂: 4 × 200 = 1 × V₂. V₂ (final volume) = 800 mL. Water to add = 800 − 200 = 600 mL.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A bottle of amoxicillin oral suspension contains 3 g of amoxicillin powder. When reconstituted correctly with water to a final volume of 100 mL, it provides 250 mg/5 mL. A parent mistakenly reconstitutes the bottle to a final volume of 200 mL. What is the resulting concentration of the suspension?
explanation: Total drug remains 3 g = 3000 mg. New concentration = 3000 mg ÷ 200 mL = 15 mg/mL = 75 mg per 5 mL. (The patient would receive only 30% of the intended dose — a serious medication error.)

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A pharmacist mixes 100 mL of a 10% w/v drug solution with 200 mL of a 4% w/v solution of the same drug. Assuming volumes are additive, what is the final concentration of the mixture?
explanation: Drug from solution 1 = (10 g/100 mL) × 100 mL = 10 g. Drug from solution 2 = (4 g/100 mL) × 200 mL = 8 g. Total drug = 18 g in 300 mL final volume. Final concentration = (18 ÷ 300) × 100 = 6% w/v.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A paediatric ward requests a digoxin oral solution at 25 micrograms/mL. The pharmacy has 20 mL of digoxin oral solution 50 micrograms/mL available. How many millilitres of water must be added to obtain the required concentration?
explanation: C₁V₁ = C₂V₂: 50 × 20 = 25 × V₂. V₂ (final volume) = 1000 ÷ 25 = 40 mL. Water to add = 40 − 20 = 20 mL.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A pharmacist needs to prepare 100 mL of an ethanol 30% v/v solution by mixing ethanol 96% v/v with ethanol 10% v/v. Using alligation, how many millilitres of the 96% v/v ethanol are required? Give your answer to two decimal places.
explanation: By alligation: parts of 96% = (30 − 10) = 20; parts of 10% = (96 − 30) = 66. Total = 86 parts = 100 mL. Volume of 96% = (20 ÷ 86) × 100 = 23.26 mL.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: An aqueous solution containing 500 mL of a 2% w/v drug is partially left uncovered overnight. By the next morning, 100 mL of water has evaporated. Assuming the drug is non-volatile, what is the resulting concentration of the solution?
explanation: Mass of drug unchanged = (2 g/100 mL) × 500 mL = 10 g. New volume = 500 − 100 = 400 mL. New concentration = (10 g ÷ 400 mL) × 100 = 2.5% w/v.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A pharmacist has 1 litre of a 10% w/v stock solution. What is the maximum volume of a 0.5% w/v solution that can be prepared from the entire stock?

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 1.5 g vial of cefuroxime is reconstituted with Water for Injections to a final volume of 15 mL. 4 mL of this solution is then added to a 100 mL bag of sodium chloride 0.9% for IV infusion. What is the final concentration of cefuroxime in the infusion bag, in mg/mL? Give your answer to two decimal places. Assume volumes are additive.
explanation: Concentration after reconstitution = 1500 mg ÷ 15 mL = 100 mg/mL. Drug in 4 mL = 400 mg. Final volume = 100 + 4 = 104 mL. Final concentration = 400 mg ÷ 104 mL = 3.846 ≈ 3.85 mg/mL.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A laboratory analyst performs a 50-fold dilution of a stock solution containing 200 micrograms/mL of a drug. What is the concentration of the diluted solution?
explanation: A 50-fold dilution divides the concentration by 50. Final concentration = 200 ÷ 50 = 4 micrograms/mL.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A specials manufacturer is compounding 30 mL of morphine sulfate 0.5% w/v oral solution for paediatric palliative care, diluting from a 2% w/v stock. How many millilitres of the 2% w/v stock are required?
explanation: C₁V₁ = C₂V₂: 2 × V₁ = 0.5 × 30. V₁ = 15 ÷ 2 = 7.5 mL of 2% stock, diluted to 30 mL final volume.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A pharmacist needs to prepare 200 mL of a drug solution at 0.025% w/v from a 5% w/v stock. What is the minimum volume, in mL, of the 5% w/v stock required?
explanation: C₁V₁ = C₂V₂: 5 × V₁ = 0.025 × 200. V₁ = 5 ÷ 5 = 1 mL. (In practice, this is too small to measure accurately, so an intermediate dilution would be used — but the calculated minimum of stock is 1 mL.)

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A hospital pharmacist is reconstituting a vial of cefotaxime 1 g for IV administration to prepare a 200 mg/mL solution. The displacement volume of cefotaxime is 0.5 mL per 1 g. How many millilitres of Water for Injections must be added to the vial to produce the required concentration?
explanation: Final volume needed = 1000 mg ÷ 200 mg/mL = 5 mL. Displacement volume of the powder = 0.5 mL. Volume of Water for Injections to add = 5 mL − 0.5 mL = 4.5 mL.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 6-year-old girl with a severe streptococcal infection is prescribed benzylpenicillin 600 mg IV. The vial contains 600 mg of benzylpenicillin powder with a displacement volume of 0.4 mL. The hospital protocol requires reconstitution to a final volume of 5 mL. How many millilitres of Water for Injections must be added?
explanation: Final volume = 5 mL. Displacement of 600 mg powder = 0.4 mL. WFI to add = 5 − 0.4 = 4.6 mL.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A flucloxacillin 250 mg vial has a displacement volume of 0.2 mL per 250 mg. The pharmacy needs a final concentration of 100 mg/mL. How many millilitres of Water for Injections should be added to the vial?
explanation: Required final volume = 250 mg ÷ 100 mg/mL = 2.5 mL. Displacement volume = 0.2 mL. WFI to add = 2.5 − 0.2 = 2.3 mL.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 1 g vial of ceftriaxone is to be reconstituted to give a final concentration of 100 mg/mL. The displacement volume of ceftriaxone is 0.8 mL per 1 g. How many millilitres of Water for Injections should be added to the vial?
explanation: Final volume = 1000 mg ÷ 100 mg/mL = 10 mL. Displacement = 0.8 mL. WFI = 10 − 0.8 = 9.2 mL.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A vancomycin 500 mg vial (displacement volume 0.3 mL) is reconstituted with Water for Injections to give a final concentration of 50 mg/mL. A patient requires a 350 mg dose. After reconstitution, what volume, in mL, should be withdrawn from the vial to give the required dose?
explanation: After reconstitution, concentration is 50 mg/mL. Volume for 350 mg = 350 ÷ 50 = 7 mL. (The displacement was used to determine reconstitution volume only; once dissolved, the working concentration is 50 mg/mL.)

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A pharmacy technician incorrectly reconstitutes a 750 mg cefuroxime vial by adding 5 mL of Water for Injections, ignoring the displacement volume. The actual displacement volume of cefuroxime is 0.6 mL per 750 mg. What is the actual concentration of the resulting solution, in mg/mL? Give your answer to two decimal places.
explanation: Final volume = WFI added + displacement = 5 + 0.6 = 5.6 mL. Concentration = 750 mg ÷ 5.6 mL = 133.93 mg/mL. (Note: the intended concentration was 750/5 = 150 mg/mL — ignoring displacement leads to under-concentration of about 11%.)

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 12 kg toddler with a urinary tract infection requires co-amoxiclav 30 mg/kg IV. A 600 mg vial of co-amoxiclav (displacement 0.5 mL per vial) is reconstituted with Water for Injections to a final volume of 6 mL. What volume, in mL, should be drawn up to administer the dose?
explanation: Dose = 30 mg/kg × 12 kg = 360 mg. Final concentration = 600 mg ÷ 6 mL = 100 mg/mL. Volume to draw = 360 mg ÷ 100 mg/mL = 3.6 mL.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: An imipenem 500 mg vial (displacement volume 0.4 mL) is reconstituted to a concentration of 50 mg/mL prior to further dilution for IV infusion. How many millilitres of Water for Injections are required to reconstitute the vial?
explanation: Required final volume = 500 mg ÷ 50 mg/mL = 10 mL. Displacement = 0.4 mL. WFI = 10 − 0.4 = 9.6 mL.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 1 g vial of vancomycin is to be reconstituted to a concentration of 100 mg/mL. The displacement volume of vancomycin is 0.07 mL per 100 mg. How many millilitres of Water for Injections must be added to the vial?
explanation: Displacement per 1 g (1000 mg) = 0.07 mL × 10 = 0.7 mL. Final volume = 1000 ÷ 100 = 10 mL. WFI = 10 − 0.7 = 9.3 mL.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A patient requires a 1.5 g dose of cefotaxime. The pharmacy has only 500 mg vials available, each with a displacement volume of 0.25 mL. The protocol requires each vial to be reconstituted to a final concentration of 100 mg/mL. What is the total volume, in mL, of Water for Injections required to reconstitute all the vials needed for this dose?
explanation: Vials needed = 1500 ÷ 500 = 3 vials. Per vial: final volume = 500 ÷ 100 = 5 mL; WFI = 5 − 0.25 = 4.75 mL. Total WFI = 4.75 × 3 = 14.25 mL.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A pharmacist adds 9.6 mL of Water for Injections to a 1 g powder vial, and the resulting solution measures exactly 10 mL. What is the displacement volume of the powder?
explanation: Displacement volume = final volume − WFI added = 10 − 9.6 = 0.4 mL per 1 g.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 1 g powder vial (displacement 0.5 mL) is reconstituted with 9.5 mL of Water for Injections, giving a final concentration of 100 mg/mL. A patient requires a 250 mg dose. What volume, in mL, should be withdrawn from the vial?
explanation: After correct reconstitution, concentration = 1000 mg ÷ 10 mL = 100 mg/mL. Volume for 250 mg = 250 ÷ 100 = 2.5 mL.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 500 mg vial of a drug with a displacement volume of 0.05 mL is reconstituted with 5 mL of Water for Injections. What is the actual concentration of the resulting solution, in mg/mL? Give your answer to two decimal places.
explanation: Final volume = 5 + 0.05 = 5.05 mL. Concentration = 500 ÷ 5.05 = 99.0099 ≈ 99.01 mg/mL.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 1.2 mega-unit (1,200,000 units) vial of benzathine benzylpenicillin is reconstituted with 3 mL of Water for Injections. The displacement volume of the powder is 1 mL. What is the final concentration of the suspension?
explanation: Final volume = 3 + 1 = 4 mL. Concentration = 1,200,000 units ÷ 4 mL = 300,000 units/mL.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A nurse mistakenly reconstitutes a 1 g powder vial (displacement 0.5 mL) with only 4 mL of Water for Injections instead of the recommended volume. What is the resulting concentration of the solution, in mg/mL? Give your answer to two decimal places.
explanation: Final volume = 4 + 0.5 = 4.5 mL. Concentration = 1000 ÷ 4.5 = 222.222 ≈ 222.22 mg/mL. (More than double the intended concentration of 100 mg/mL — could lead to a serious overdose.)

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A patient requires a 750 mg dose of an antibiotic at a final concentration of 100 mg/mL. The pharmacy uses a single 1 g vial (displacement volume 0.7 mL). How many millilitres of Water for Injections are required to reconstitute the vial?
explanation: Required final volume to give 100 mg/mL from a 1 g vial = 1000 ÷ 100 = 10 mL. Displacement = 0.7 mL. WFI = 10 − 0.7 = 9.3 mL. (7.5 mL is then withdrawn to give the 750 mg dose.)

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: The package insert for a 750 mg vial of an antibiotic powder states that adding 6.5 mL of Water for Injections produces a solution with a final concentration of 100 mg/mL. What is the displacement volume of the powder?
explanation: Required final volume = 750 mg ÷ 100 mg/mL = 7.5 mL. WFI added = 6.5 mL. Displacement = final volume − WFI = 7.5 − 6.5 = 1.0 mL.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 500 mg powder vial (displacement 0.3 mL) is reconstituted to give 100 mg/mL, then the entire reconstituted volume is added to a 100 mL bag of sodium chloride 0.9% for IV infusion. Assuming volumes are additive, what is the final concentration of the drug in the infusion bag, in mg/mL? Give your answer to two decimal places.
explanation: Reconstituted volume = 500 ÷ 100 = 5 mL. Add to 100 mL bag → final volume = 105 mL. Final concentration = 500 mg ÷ 105 mL = 4.7619 ≈ 4.76 mg/mL.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 1.2 g co-amoxiclav vial has a displacement volume of 0.9 mL. The protocol requires reconstitution to 60 mg/mL. A nurse incorrectly adds 20 mL of Water for Injections (ignoring displacement) and then withdraws 10 mL for a patient. How many milligrams of co-amoxiclav are actually administered? Give your answer to the nearest whole number.
explanation: Actual final volume = 20 + 0.9 = 20.9 mL. Actual concentration = 1200 ÷ 20.9 = 57.42 mg/mL. Drug in 10 mL withdrawn = 57.42 × 10 = 574.16 ≈ 574 mg. (The intended dose was 600 mg — the patient receives ~4% less than prescribed.)

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: An 8 kg infant with meningitis requires ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg IV. The pharmacy reconstitutes a 1 g ceftriaxone vial (displacement volume 0.8 mL) with Water for Injections to give a final concentration of 100 mg/mL. What volume, in mL, of the reconstituted solution should be drawn up for the dose?
explanation: Dose = 50 mg/kg × 8 kg = 400 mg. Final concentration = 100 mg/mL. Volume to draw = 400 ÷ 100 = 4 mL. (The displacement of 0.8 mL informed that 9.2 mL WFI was needed for reconstitution to 10 mL total volume.)

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 72-year-old man weighing 80 kg is admitted with sepsis and prescribed an IV infusion of noradrenaline at 0.1 micrograms/kg/minute. The infusion bag contains noradrenaline 8 mg in 50 mL of glucose 5%. At what rate, in mL/hour, should the infusion be administered? Give your answer to one decimal place.
explanation: Dose required = 0.1 micrograms/kg/min × 80 kg = 8 micrograms/min = 480 micrograms/hour = 0.48 mg/hour. Bag concentration = 8 mg ÷ 50 mL = 0.16 mg/mL. Infusion rate = 0.48 ÷ 0.16 = 3.0 mL/hour.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A patient is prescribed a 1 litre IV infusion of sodium chloride 0.9% to be administered over 8 hours via a gravity giving set. The giving set delivers 20 drops per millilitre. At what rate, in drops per minute, should the infusion be administered? Give your answer to the nearest whole number.
explanation: Flow rate = 1000 mL ÷ 8 hours = 125 mL/hour. Per minute: 125 ÷ 60 = 2.083 mL/min. Drops per minute = 2.083 × 20 = 41.67 ≈ 42 drops/min.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 68-year-old man with a pulmonary embolism is prescribed an IV infusion of unfractionated heparin at 1200 units/hour. The pharmacy supplies a syringe containing heparin sodium 25,000 units in 50 mL of sodium chloride 0.9%. At what rate, in mL/hour, should the syringe pump be set? Give your answer to one decimal place.
explanation: Concentration = 25,000 units ÷ 50 mL = 500 units/mL. Rate = 1200 units/hour ÷ 500 units/mL = 2.4 mL/hour.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 54-year-old woman with diabetic ketoacidosis is started on a fixed-rate intravenous insulin infusion at 0.1 units/kg/hour. She weighs 60 kg. The pharmacy supplies a 50 mL syringe containing 50 units of soluble insulin (Actrapid) made up to 50 mL with sodium chloride 0.9%. At what rate, in mL/hour, should the syringe pump be set?
explanation: Insulin dose = 0.1 units/kg/hour × 60 kg = 6 units/hour. Concentration = 50 units ÷ 50 mL = 1 unit/mL. Rate = 6 units/hour ÷ 1 unit/mL = 6 mL/hour.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 22 kg child requires maintenance IV fluids using the 4-2-1 rule: 4 mL/kg/hour for the first 10 kg, 2 mL/kg/hour for the next 10 kg, and 1 mL/kg/hour for each kilogram thereafter. At what rate, in mL/hour, should the maintenance fluid infusion be administered?
explanation: First 10 kg: 4 × 10 = 40 mL/hour. Next 10 kg: 2 × 10 = 20 mL/hour. Remaining 2 kg: 1 × 2 = 2 mL/hour. Total = 40 + 20 + 2 = 62 mL/hour.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 15 kg child is prescribed normal immunoglobulin 10% w/v at a total dose of 0.5 g/kg. The infusion is given at the following stepped rates: 0.3 mL/kg/hour for 30 minutes, then 0.6 mL/kg/hour for 30 minutes, then 0.9 mL/kg/hour for 30 minutes, then 1.2 mL/kg/hour to complete the infusion. What is the total infusion duration, in minutes? Give your answer to the nearest whole number.
explanation: Total dose = 0.5 g/kg × 15 kg = 7.5 g. At 10% w/v (100 mg/mL), total volume = 7.5 g ÷ 0.1 g/mL = 75 mL. Volume delivered in first 90 min: (0.3×15×0.5) + (0.6×15×0.5) + (0.9×15×0.5) = 2.25 + 4.5 + 6.75 = 13.5 mL. Remaining = 75 − 13.5 = 61.5 mL. Final rate = 1.2 × 15 = 18 mL/hour. Final stage time = 61.5 ÷ 18 = 3.4167 hours = 205 min. Total = 90 + 205 = 295 minutes.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 70 kg man with an acute severe asthma exacerbation is prescribed an IV aminophylline maintenance infusion at 0.5 mg/kg/hour. The pharmacy supplies aminophylline 500 mg in a 500 mL bag of sodium chloride 0.9%. At what rate, in mL/hour, should the infusion be administered?
explanation: Dose = 0.5 mg/kg/hour × 70 kg = 35 mg/hour. Bag concentration = 500 mg ÷ 500 mL = 1 mg/mL. Rate = 35 mg/hour ÷ 1 mg/mL = 35 mL/hour.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 75 kg patient in cardiogenic shock is prescribed dopamine at 5 micrograms/kg/minute. The infusion bag contains dopamine 200 mg in 250 mL of glucose 5%. At what rate, in mL/hour, should the infusion be administered? Give your answer to two decimal places.
explanation: Dose = 5 micrograms/kg/min × 75 kg = 375 micrograms/min = 22,500 micrograms/hour = 22.5 mg/hour. Concentration = 200 mg ÷ 250 mL = 0.8 mg/mL. Rate = 22.5 ÷ 0.8 = 28.125 ≈ 28.13 mL/hour.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 56-year-old woman with a hypertensive emergency is receiving an IV labetalol infusion at 30 mL/hour. The bag contains labetalol 200 mg in 200 mL of glucose 5%. What dose, in mg/hour, is the patient receiving?
explanation: Concentration = 200 mg ÷ 200 mL = 1 mg/mL. Dose = 30 mL/hour × 1 mg/mL = 30 mg/hour.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A patient is receiving a 500 mL infusion of glucose 5% at 80 mL/hour. How many minutes will it take to deliver the full bag? Give your answer to the nearest whole minute.
explanation: Time = volume ÷ rate = 500 mL ÷ 80 mL/hour = 6.25 hours = 6.25 × 60 = 375 minutes.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 32-year-old woman with eclampsia requires a loading dose of magnesium sulfate 4 g IV over 20 minutes. The pharmacy prepares the dose by diluting magnesium sulfate to a final volume of 100 mL with sodium chloride 0.9%. At what rate, in mL/hour, should the infusion be administered?
explanation: Rate = volume ÷ time = 100 mL ÷ 20 min = 5 mL/min = 5 × 60 = 300 mL/hour.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 65-year-old patient with acute coronary syndrome is prescribed an IV glyceryl trinitrate (GTN) infusion at 25 micrograms/minute. The pharmacy supplies a 50 mL syringe containing GTN 50 mg in glucose 5%. At what rate, in mL/hour, should the syringe pump be set?
explanation: Concentration = 50 mg ÷ 50 mL = 1 mg/mL = 1000 micrograms/mL. Dose = 25 micrograms/min = 1500 micrograms/hour = 1.5 mg/hour. Rate = 1.5 mg/hour ÷ 1 mg/mL = 1.5 mL/hour.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A patient is to receive 1 unit of packed red blood cells (volume 300 mL) over 4 hours. The blood giving set delivers 15 drops per mL. At what rate, in drops per minute, should the transfusion be administered? Give your answer to the nearest whole number.
explanation: Flow rate = 300 mL ÷ 4 hours = 75 mL/hour = 1.25 mL/min. Drops per minute = 1.25 × 15 = 18.75 ≈ 19 drops/min.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A patient receives a dobutamine infusion at 5 mL/hour for 6 hours, then the rate is increased to 8 mL/hour for a further 4 hours. The infusion bag contains dobutamine 250 mg in 50 mL of glucose 5%. What is the total dose of dobutamine, in mg, that the patient has received over the 10 hours?
explanation: Concentration = 250 mg ÷ 50 mL = 5 mg/mL. Dose hours 0–6: 5 mL/hour × 5 mg/mL × 6 hours = 150 mg. Dose hours 6–10: 8 mL/hour × 5 mg/mL × 4 hours = 160 mg. Total = 150 + 160 = 310 mg.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 60 kg woman is prescribed vancomycin 15 mg/kg IV, to be given over 90 minutes. The pharmacy dilutes the dose in 250 mL of sodium chloride 0.9%. At what rate, in mL/hour, should the infusion be administered? Give your answer to two decimal places.
explanation: Rate = volume ÷ time = 250 mL ÷ 1.5 hours = 166.667 ≈ 166.67 mL/hour. (The dose is 60 × 15 = 900 mg, but this is delivered by emptying the 250 mL bag — the dose calculation is a check, not the rate calculation.)

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A patient-controlled analgesia (PCA) pump contains morphine sulfate 50 mg in 50 mL of sodium chloride 0.9%. The pump is programmed with a continuous background rate of 0.5 mg/hour, plus a 1 mg bolus on demand with a maximum of 4 boluses per hour. What is the maximum dose, in mg, of morphine the patient could receive in 1 hour?
explanation: Background = 0.5 mg/hour. Maximum boluses = 4 × 1 mg = 4 mg/hour. Maximum total = 0.5 + 4 = 4.5 mg/hour.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 1 litre bag of Hartmann's solution is started at 14:30 at a rate of 125 mL/hour. At what clock time will the infusion be completed?
explanation: Duration = 1000 mL ÷ 125 mL/hour = 8 hours. Finish time = 14:30 + 8 hours = 22:30.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A patient with refractory heart failure is prescribed an IV furosemide infusion at 4 mg/hour. The pharmacy supplies a 25 mL syringe containing furosemide 250 mg. At what rate, in mL/hour, should the syringe pump be set?
explanation: Concentration = 250 mg ÷ 25 mL = 10 mg/mL. Rate = 4 mg/hour ÷ 10 mg/mL = 0.4 mL/hour.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: An IV bag contains potassium chloride 40 mmol in 1 litre of sodium chloride 0.9%. The maximum safe peripheral infusion rate of potassium is 10 mmol/hour. What is the maximum rate, in mL/hour, at which this bag can be infused via a peripheral line?
explanation: Bag concentration = 40 mmol ÷ 1000 mL = 0.04 mmol/mL. Max rate = 10 mmol/hour ÷ 0.04 mmol/mL = 250 mL/hour. (Higher rates require central venous access and cardiac monitoring.)

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 60 kg woman with acute ischaemic stroke is prescribed alteplase 0.9 mg/kg IV (maximum 90 mg). 10% of the total dose is given as a bolus over 1 minute, and the remaining 90% is given as an infusion over 60 minutes. What is the rate, in mg/hour, of the maintenance infusion? Give your answer to one decimal place.
explanation: Total dose = 0.9 × 60 = 54 mg. Bolus = 10% × 54 = 5.4 mg. Maintenance = 54 − 5.4 = 48.6 mg over 60 min = 48.6 mg/hour.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A randomised controlled trial of a new statin for primary prevention of cardiovascular events reports the following 5-year event rates: control group 8.4%, treatment group 5.6%. The formula for absolute risk reduction (ARR) is ARR = CER − EER. The formula for number needed to treat (NNT) is NNT = 1 ÷ ARR. What is the minimum number of patients that need to be treated for 5 years to prevent one cardiovascular event? Round up to the nearest whole patient.
explanation: ARR = 0.084 − 0.056 = 0.028. NNT = 1 ÷ 0.028 = 35.71. Always round NNT UP (cannot treat a fraction of a patient), giving NNT = 36.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: In a randomised controlled trial, 20% of patients in the control group experienced a stroke, compared with 15% of patients in the treatment group. What is the relative risk reduction (RRR) of stroke?

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A trial of a new anticoagulant reports a major bleeding rate of 4% in the treatment group compared with 1% in the placebo group. The formula for absolute risk increase (ARI) is ARI = EER − CER. The formula for number needed to harm (NNH) is NNH = 1 ÷ ARI. What is the NNH for major bleeding? Round up to the nearest whole patient.
explanation: ARI = 0.04 − 0.01 = 0.03. NNH = 1 ÷ 0.03 = 33.33. Round up to next whole patient = 34.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: In a clinical trial, 68 patients out of 3260 reported headache after taking a new oral contraceptive. The MHRA frequency categories are: Very common (>1 in 10), Common (1 in 100 to 1 in 10), Uncommon (1 in 1000 to 1 in 100), Rare (1 in 10,000 to 1 in 1000), Very rare (<1 in 10,000). Which is the most appropriate description of the frequency of headache reported?
explanation: Frequency = 68 ÷ 3260 = 0.0209 = 2.09% = approximately 1 in 48. This falls within the Common range (1 in 100 to 1 in 10).

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: In a case-control study investigating the link between a medication and a rare cancer, the following data is collected: 40 of the cancer cases were exposed to the medication and 60 were unexposed; 30 of the controls were exposed and 70 were unexposed. The formula for odds ratio is: OR = (a × d) ÷ (b × c), where a = exposed cases, b = unexposed cases, c = exposed controls, d = unexposed controls. What is the odds ratio? Give your answer to two decimal places.
explanation: OR = (40 × 70) ÷ (60 × 30) = 2800 ÷ 1800 = 1.5556 ≈ 1.56. (An OR > 1 suggests increased odds of exposure among cases.)

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A new screening test for colorectal cancer is evaluated in 200 patients. Of the 100 patients with confirmed cancer, the test correctly identified 80. Of the 100 patients without cancer, the test correctly identified 90. The formula for sensitivity is: Sensitivity = True positives ÷ (True positives + False negatives). What is the sensitivity of the test?
explanation: True positives = 80. False negatives = 100 − 80 = 20. Sensitivity = 80 ÷ (80 + 20) = 80 ÷ 100 = 0.80 = 80%.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: Using the same screening test data: 80 of 100 cancer patients tested positive, and 90 of 100 cancer-free patients tested negative. The formula for specificity is: Specificity = True negatives ÷ (True negatives + False positives). What is the specificity of the test?
explanation: True negatives = 90. False positives = 100 − 90 = 10. Specificity = 90 ÷ (90 + 10) = 90 ÷ 100 = 0.90 = 90%.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: Using the screening test data (80 true positives, 90 true negatives, 10 false positives, 20 false negatives), what is the positive predictive value (PPV) of the test? PPV = True positives ÷ (True positives + False positives). Give your answer to two decimal places.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: Post-marketing surveillance reports 12 cases of agranulocytosis in 50,000 patients prescribed a new antipsychotic. Using the MHRA frequency categories (Very common >1 in 10, Common 1 in 100 to 1 in 10, Uncommon 1 in 1000 to 1 in 100, Rare 1 in 10,000 to 1 in 1000, Very rare <1 in 10,000), how should this frequency be described?
explanation: Frequency = 12 ÷ 50,000 = 0.00024 = approximately 1 in 4167. This falls within the Rare range (1 in 10,000 to 1 in 1000).

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A clinical trial reports that a new SGLT2 inhibitor has a hazard ratio of 0.65 (95% CI 0.55–0.78) for major adverse cardiovascular events compared with placebo. By what percentage is the hazard of a major adverse cardiovascular event reduced by the new drug, compared with placebo?
explanation: Relative reduction = 1 − HR = 1 − 0.65 = 0.35 = 35%. (A hazard ratio below 1 indicates a reduced hazard with the intervention.)

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A cohort study reports that patients taking a new analgesic have a relative risk of myocardial infarction of 1.50 (95% confidence interval 0.95 to 2.20) compared with non-users. How should this result be interpreted in terms of statistical significance?
option_a: Statistically significant because the relative risk is greater than 1.0
option_b: Statistically significant because the confidence interval is greater than 0.5
option_c: Not statistically significant because the confidence interval crosses 1.0
option_d: Not statistically significant because the relative risk is less than 2.0
option_e: Not statistically significant because the upper limit is below 3.0
correct_answer: Not statistically significant because the confidence interval crosses 1.0
explanation: For ratio measures (RR, OR, HR), a result is statistically significant at p<0.05 only if the 95% confidence interval does NOT cross 1.0. Here the CI is 0.95 to 2.20 — it includes 1.0, so the result is not statistically significant.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A trial of a new statin reports that the 5-year incidence of myocardial infarction was 12% in the control group and 8% in the treatment group. How many patients need to be treated for 5 years to prevent one myocardial infarction? Round up to the nearest whole patient.
explanation: ARR = 0.12 − 0.08 = 0.04. NNT = 1 ÷ 0.04 = 25 patients (no rounding needed).

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: In a randomised controlled trial of a new vaccine, 75 cases of disease occurred in the placebo group of 1500 participants. What is the control event rate (CER), expressed as a percentage?
explanation: CER = number of events ÷ number in control group = 75 ÷ 1500 = 0.05 = 5%.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A trial of a new antiplatelet drug for secondary prevention of stroke has 1200 participants randomised equally between treatment and placebo. Over 2 years, 30 strokes occurred in the 600 treatment patients and 60 strokes occurred in the 600 placebo patients. What is the number needed to treat (NNT) over 2 years to prevent one stroke?

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A patient's blood pressure (systolic) is recorded on five separate visits as: 120, 122, 118, 124, and 116 mmHg. What is the mean systolic blood pressure?

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: Post-marketing data report 8 cases of severe hepatotoxicity in 4000 patients prescribed a new oral antifungal. What is the rate of hepatotoxicity per 1000 patients prescribed?

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A medication has two known adverse effects: nausea (occurring in 5% of patients) and headache (occurring in 2% of patients). Assuming the two events are independent, what is the probability that a patient will experience at least one of these adverse effects? Give your answer to one decimal place.
explanation: P(no nausea) = 0.95; P(no headache) = 0.98. P(neither) = 0.95 × 0.98 = 0.931. P(at least one) = 1 − 0.931 = 0.069 = 6.9%.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A pharmacy records the number of medication errors reported per month over 7 months: 5, 12, 8, 25, 18, 15, 20. What is the median number of monthly medication errors?
explanation: First arrange the data in order: 5, 8, 12, 15, 18, 20, 25. With 7 values, the median is the 4th value = 15.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: Two clinical trials testing the same drug report different p-values: Trial A reports p = 0.04, and Trial B reports p = 0.001. Which trial provides stronger statistical evidence against the null hypothesis?
option_a: Trial A, because the p-value is closer to 0.05
option_b: Trial A, because the p-value is greater than 0.001
option_c: Trial B, because the p-value is smaller
option_d: Trial B, because the p-value is greater than 0.05
option_e: They provide equal evidence because both are below 0.05
correct_answer: Trial B, because the p-value is smaller
explanation: A smaller p-value provides stronger evidence against the null hypothesis. p = 0.001 means there is a 0.1% chance the observed result could have occurred under the null hypothesis, compared with 4% for p = 0.04 — so Trial B is more statistically significant.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A new drug has an NNT of 50 over 5 years to prevent one cardiovascular death. The drug costs £200 per patient per year. What is the total cost, in pounds, to prevent one cardiovascular death over the 5-year period?
explanation: Cost per patient over 5 years = £200 × 5 = £1000. Cost per event prevented = NNT × cost per patient = 50 × £1000 = £50,000.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 55-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes is prescribed empagliflozin 10 mg tablets, one to be taken once daily. The drug tariff price is £36.59 per pack of 28 tablets. The pharmacy receives a 12.5% discount on the list price, and the dispensing fee per item is £1.27. What is the net cost to the NHS for a 28-day supply of empagliflozin? Give your answer in pounds to two decimal places.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A new chemotherapy regimen costs £30,000 per patient and generates an estimated 1.5 additional quality-adjusted life years (QALYs) compared with standard care. What is the incremental cost per QALY gained?
explanation: Cost per QALY = total incremental cost ÷ QALYs gained = £30,000 ÷ 1.5 = £20,000 per QALY. (NICE typically considers interventions cost-effective at <£20,000–£30,000 per QALY.)

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 42-year-old man is prescribed isoniazid 300 mg PO daily for 26 weeks for tuberculosis. He requires a liquid special. Isoniazid is available as a 50 mg/5 mL oral solution in 500 mL bottles costing £30.66 each. Bottles must be dispensed whole (cannot be split). What is the total cost, in pounds, to purchase 26 weeks of treatment?
explanation: Daily volume = (300 mg ÷ 50 mg) × 5 mL = 30 mL. Total volume = 30 mL × 7 days × 26 weeks = 5460 mL. Bottles needed = 5460 ÷ 500 = 10.92, round up to 11 bottles. Total cost = 11 × £30.66 = £337.26.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A clinical commissioning group is comparing two equally effective treatment options for chronic pain: Drug A costs £15 per day, and Drug B costs £400 per 30-day pack. Per month (30 days), what is the cost saving per patient if Drug B is used instead of Drug A?
explanation: Drug A: £15 × 30 days = £450 per month. Drug B: £400 per month. Saving = £450 − £400 = £50 per patient per month.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A CCG has 5000 patients on chronic pain therapy. Switching all of them from Drug A to Drug B would save £50 per patient per month. What is the total annual saving across the CCG?
explanation: Monthly saving = 5000 patients × £50 = £250,000. Annual saving = £250,000 × 12 months = £3,000,000.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A pack of 60 tablets of a new antihypertensive costs £45. The dose is one tablet twice daily. What is the cost per day of treatment, in pounds?
explanation: Cost per tablet = £45 ÷ 60 = £0.75. Cost per day (BD) = £0.75 × 2 = £1.50.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A new biologic for rheumatoid arthritis costs £12,000 per year compared with £4,000 per year for standard therapy. The new biologic generates 0.4 additional QALYs per patient per year. What is the incremental cost-effectiveness ratio (ICER) per QALY gained?
explanation: Incremental cost = £12,000 − £4,000 = £8,000. ICER = incremental cost ÷ incremental QALYs = £8,000 ÷ 0.4 = £20,000 per QALY.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A patient has been taking a branded statin (£25 per 28 tablets, OD) for 1 year. The GP is considering switching to the generic equivalent (£4 per 28 tablets). What is the annual cost saving (assume 365-day year), in pounds, if the patient is switched? Give your answer to the nearest pound.
explanation: Brand cost per day = £25 ÷ 28 = £0.8929. Generic cost per day = £4 ÷ 28 = £0.1429. Daily saving = £0.75. Annual saving = £0.75 × 365 = £273.75 ≈ £274.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A patient is prescribed a 7-day course of an antibiotic, one capsule three times a day. The capsules are supplied in packs of 28 only and cost £8.50 per pack. Whole packs must be dispensed. What is the cost to the NHS of supplying this course?
explanation: Capsules required = 1 × 3 × 7 = 21 capsules. Packs needed = ceiling(21 ÷ 28) = 1 pack. Cost = 1 × £8.50 = £8.50.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A patient is on a monthly repeat prescription for a medication that costs £8.50 per pack of 28 tablets. Each repeat dispenses one pack. What is the total medicine cost (excluding fees) for 12 months of repeat prescriptions?
explanation: Annual cost = 12 packs × £8.50 = £102.00.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A primary care network is considering switching 1000 patients from Drug A (£800 per patient per year) to Drug B (£950 per patient per year). What is the budget impact, in pounds, of switching all 1000 patients for 1 year?
explanation: Cost increase per patient = £950 − £800 = £150. Total budget impact = 1000 × £150 = £150,000 increase.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A new oncology drug has an incremental cost-effectiveness ratio (ICER) of £35,000 per QALY gained. NICE generally considers interventions cost-effective if the ICER is below £20,000–£30,000 per QALY (with some flexibility for end-of-life treatments). Based on this ICER alone, how should this drug be classified?
explanation: An ICER of £35,000/QALY exceeds the standard NICE threshold range of £20,000–£30,000/QALY. NICE would typically not recommend it for routine NHS use unless special circumstances apply (e.g. end-of-life criteria, very rare disease).

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A pharmacy dispenses a Category M drug with a Drug Tariff list price of £20.00. The NHS applies a clawback discount of 7% on this category. What is the reimbursement amount, in pounds, the pharmacy will receive for the medicine cost?
explanation: Reimbursement = list price × (1 − clawback %) = £20.00 × (1 − 0.07) = £20.00 × 0.93 = £18.60.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A new antifungal drug costs £500 per course, with a treatment success rate of 80%. What is the cost per patient successfully treated, in pounds?
explanation: Cost per success = total cost per patient ÷ success rate = £500 ÷ 0.80 = £625. (This accounts for the cost of treating all patients, including those for whom treatment fails.)

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A patient with type 2 diabetes uses insulin glargine 300 units/mL pre-filled pens (1.5 mL pen = 450 units). She uses 36 units once daily. The pens are supplied in packs of 5 (2250 units total) at a cost of £45 per pack. Whole packs must be dispensed. How many packs are required for 365 days of treatment, and what is the annual cost?
explanation: Annual usage = 36 units × 365 days = 13,140 units. Packs needed = 13,140 ÷ 2250 = 5.84, round up to 6 packs. Cost = 6 × £45 = £270.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A clinical pharmacist is employed by a hospital at a daily cost of £400. By undertaking medicines reconciliation, the pharmacist prevents an average of 5 medication-related admissions per day, each costing the NHS £200 in additional care. What is the net daily saving generated by the pharmacist's role?
explanation: Daily savings prevented = 5 × £200 = £1000. Net daily saving = £1000 − £400 (pharmacist cost) = £600 saving.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 35-year-old man with HIV is initiated on a once-daily antiretroviral regimen costing £100 per month. He is expected to take this medication for the rest of his life (estimated 30 years). What is the total undiscounted lifetime medication cost, in pounds?
explanation: Annual cost = £100 × 12 = £1200. Lifetime cost = £1200 × 30 years = £36,000. (In real cost-effectiveness analyses, future costs are discounted, typically at 3.5% per year per NICE guidance.)

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A new chemotherapy regimen costs an additional £40,000 per patient compared with standard care, and extends life expectancy by an average of 4 years. What is the incremental cost per life-year gained?
explanation: Cost per life-year gained = incremental cost ÷ incremental life-years = £40,000 ÷ 4 = £10,000.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A patient is prescribed a 30-day course of one tablet three times a day. The medication is supplied in original packs of 28 tablets only, costing £6 per pack. Whole packs must be dispensed. How many packs should be dispensed and what is the total cost?
explanation: Tablets needed = 1 × 3 × 30 = 90 tablets. Packs needed = ceiling(90 ÷ 28) = 4 packs (112 tablets). Cost = 4 × £6 = £24.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A pharmacy sells an OTC product for £10.00 (VAT-inclusive retail price). VAT is charged at the standard rate of 20%. What is the price excluding VAT, in pounds? Give your answer to two decimal places.
explanation: Price excluding VAT = retail price ÷ (1 + VAT rate) = £10.00 ÷ 1.20 = £8.333 ≈ £8.33. (Common error: subtracting 20% from £10 gives £8.00, but VAT is added on top of the ex-VAT price, so this is incorrect.)

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 65-year-old man is being treated with IV gentamicin. He has an estimated volume of distribution of 18 L and an elimination half-life of 4 hours. The formula for clearance is: Cl = (0.693 × Vd) ÷ t½. What is the gentamicin clearance, in L/hour, for this patient? Give your answer to two decimal places.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A new investigational drug has a half-life of 16 hours and follows first-order kinetics. How many hours will it take for a patient's blood concentration of the drug to fall to 6.25% of the initial concentration?
explanation: 6.25% = 1/16 = (1/2)⁴, which means 4 half-lives have passed. Time = 4 × 16 = 64 hours.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A patient stops taking a long-acting drug with a half-life of 24 hours. After how many days will the plasma concentration fall to approximately 25% of its peak level?
explanation: 25% = 1/4 = (1/2)², representing 2 half-lives. Time = 2 × 24 hours = 48 hours = 2 days.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A patient is started on a new oral antihypertensive with an elimination half-life of 8 hours. Approximately how many hours will it take to reach 97% of steady-state plasma concentration with regular dosing? Use the convention that steady state is achieved after 5 half-lives.
explanation: Steady state is conventionally reached after approximately 5 half-lives. Time = 5 × 8 = 40 hours. (After 5 t½: 96.875% of steady state, often quoted as 97%.)

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 70 kg patient is given an IV bolus dose of 100 mg of a drug. The peak plasma concentration measured immediately after administration is 5 mg/L. The formula is: Vd = Dose ÷ Cp. What is the volume of distribution of the drug, in litres?
explanation: Vd = Dose ÷ Cp = 100 mg ÷ 5 mg/L = 20 L.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A patient requires a loading dose of an IV antiarrhythmic to achieve a target plasma concentration of 10 mg/L. The drug has a volume of distribution of 35 L. The formula is: Loading dose = Cp(target) × Vd. What is the loading dose, in mg?
explanation: Loading dose = target Cp × Vd = 10 mg/L × 35 L = 350 mg.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A patient receiving an IV theophylline infusion has a measured drug clearance of 5 L/hour. The target plasma concentration is 4 mg/L. The formula for maintenance infusion rate at steady state is: MD rate = Cl × Cp(target). What is the required maintenance infusion rate, in mg/hour?
explanation: Maintenance dose rate = Cl × target Cp = 5 L/hour × 4 mg/L = 20 mg/hour.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 78-year-old woman weighing 60 kg has a serum creatinine of 140 micromol/L. Use the Cockcroft-Gault equation: CrCl (mL/min) = [(140 − age) × weight × 1.04] ÷ serum creatinine. What is her estimated creatinine clearance, in mL/min? Give your answer to one decimal place.
explanation: CrCl = [(140 − 78) × 60 × 1.04] ÷ 140 = (62 × 60 × 1.04) ÷ 140 = 3868.8 ÷ 140 = 27.63 ≈ 27.6 mL/min. (CrCl <30 mL/min indicates severe renal impairment.)

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 65-year-old man weighing 75 kg has a serum creatinine of 110 micromol/L. Use the Cockcroft-Gault equation for males: CrCl (mL/min) = [(140 − age) × weight × 1.23] ÷ serum creatinine. What is his estimated creatinine clearance, in mL/min? Give your answer to one decimal place.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 1-year-old boy who is 70 cm tall has a serum creatinine of 77 micromol/L. The paediatric eGFR formula is: eGFR (mL/min/1.73m²) = (35 × height in cm) ÷ serum creatinine in micromol/L. What is his estimated GFR, in mL/min/1.73m²? Give your answer to one decimal place.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: Continuing the previous case: the 1-year-old boy weighs 8 kg and is prescribed atenolol 1 mg/kg PO daily in 2 divided doses. The dose should be reduced to 50% of usual if eGFR is 10–35 mL/min/1.73m². Based on his calculated eGFR of 31.8 mL/min/1.73m², how many mg of atenolol are required per dose?
explanation: Usual daily dose = 1 mg/kg × 8 kg = 8 mg/day = 4 mg per dose (BD). eGFR 31.8 falls in 10–35 range → use 50% of usual = 2 mg per dose.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A patient is taking an oral drug at 200 mg every 12 hours. The drug clearance is 4 L/hour. Assuming complete absorption, the formula for average steady-state concentration is: Cp(ss) = Dose ÷ (Cl × τ), where τ is the dosing interval. What is the average steady-state plasma concentration, in mg/L? Give your answer to two decimal places.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A bioavailability study compares oral and IV administration of equal doses of a drug. The area under the curve (AUC) following oral administration is 60 mg·hour/L, and following IV administration is 100 mg·hour/L. What is the absolute bioavailability of the oral formulation?
explanation: Absolute bioavailability F = AUC(oral) ÷ AUC(IV) (for equal doses). F = 60 ÷ 100 = 0.60 = 60%.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A drug has a half-life of 6 hours and follows first-order kinetics. After how many hours will 87.5% of the initial dose have been eliminated from the body?
explanation: 87.5% eliminated = 12.5% remaining = 1/8 = (1/2)³ → 3 half-lives. Time = 3 × 6 hours = 18 hours.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A patient is to be started on an oral drug with a target plasma concentration of 8 mg/L. The drug has a volume of distribution of 50 L and an oral bioavailability of 70%. The formula for an oral loading dose is: LD = (Cp(target) × Vd) ÷ F. What is the required loading dose, in mg? Give your answer to the nearest whole number.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A drug has a clearance of 6 L/hour and a volume of distribution of 24 L. Using the formula t½ = (0.693 × Vd) ÷ Cl, what is the elimination half-life of the drug, in hours? Give your answer to two decimal places.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A patient receives a once-daily dose of gentamicin and the peak plasma concentration is 8 mg/L. The patient's gentamicin half-life is 3 hours. What is the minimum time, in hours, that must elapse from the peak before the trough concentration falls to 2 mg/L?
explanation: 8 mg/L → 4 mg/L (1 half-life) → 2 mg/L (2 half-lives). Time = 2 × 3 = 6 hours.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: An 82-year-old woman weighing 58 kg with atrial fibrillation has a serum creatinine of 145 micromol/L. She is being initiated on apixaban for stroke prevention. The standard dose is 5 mg twice daily, but the dose is reduced to 2.5 mg twice daily if the patient meets at least 2 of the following 3 criteria: age ≥80 years, body weight ≤60 kg, serum creatinine ≥133 micromol/L. Which dose is appropriate for this patient?
explanation: Patient meets all 3 criteria: age 82 (≥80), weight 58 kg (≤60), SCr 145 (≥133). With ≥2 criteria met, the dose is reduced to 2.5 mg BD.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 70-year-old man weighing 80 kg has a serum creatinine of 180 micromol/L. He is to be prescribed a renally cleared antibiotic with a standard dose of 400 mg. Use the Cockcroft-Gault equation for males: CrCl (mL/min) = [(140 − age) × weight × 1.23] ÷ serum creatinine. Dose adjustments are: 100% of usual dose if CrCl >50 mL/min; 75% if CrCl 30–50 mL/min; 50% if CrCl 15–29 mL/min. What is the adjusted dose for this patient, in mg?

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 50-year-old man is 6 feet (72 inches) tall. The Devine formula for ideal body weight (IBW) in males is: IBW (kg) = 50 + 2.3 × (height in inches − 60). What is his ideal body weight, in kg? Give your answer to one decimal place.
explanation: IBW = 50 + 2.3 × (72 − 60) = 50 + 2.3 × 12 = 50 + 27.6 = 77.6 kg. (For obese patients, IBW is often used in place of total body weight for dosing aminoglycosides and certain other drugs.)

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A prescription is received for prednisolone 5 mg tablets for a 58-year-old man with a flare of polymyalgia rheumatica. The dose is to be reduced gradually as follows: 30 mg once daily for 2 weeks, then 25 mg once daily for 2 weeks, then 20 mg once daily for 2 weeks, then 15 mg once daily for 4 weeks. The pharmacy stocks prednisolone 5 mg tablets only. How many prednisolone 5 mg tablets are required to complete this course?
explanation: Daily tablet count: 30 mg = 6, 25 mg = 5, 20 mg = 4, 15 mg = 3. Tablets per stage: (6×14) + (5×14) + (4×14) + (3×28) = 84 + 70 + 56 + 84 = 294 tablets.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: An 11-year-old boy who weighs 35 kg has juvenile idiopathic arthritis. He is prescribed sulfasalazine according to the following regimen: 5 mg/kg twice daily for 1 week, then 10 mg/kg twice daily for 1 week, then 20 mg/kg twice daily for 1 week, then maintenance 25 mg/kg twice daily. How many mL of sulfasalazine 250 mg/5 mL oral suspension are required for the first 4 weeks of treatment?

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 16-year-old patient is started on levetiracetam for focal seizures. The titration regimen is: 250 mg (1 tablet) once a day for 1 week; then 250 mg (1 tablet) twice a day for 2 weeks; then 500 mg (2 tablets) twice a day for 2 weeks; then 750 mg (3 tablets) twice a day ongoing. How many levetiracetam 250 mg tablets are required for the first 8 weeks of treatment?
explanation: Week 1: 1 × 7 = 7 tabs. Weeks 2–3: 2 × 14 = 28 tabs. Weeks 4–5: 4 × 14 = 56 tabs. Weeks 6–8: 6 × 21 = 126 tabs. Total = 7 + 28 + 56 + 126 = 217 tablets.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 4-year-old girl who weighs 17 kg has epilepsy. She is prescribed clobazam 250 micrograms/kg PO twice daily. Clobazam 10 mg/5 mL oral suspension is available, supplied with a 5 mL oral syringe with 0.5 mL graduations. Each dose should be rounded to the nearest 0.5 mL. How many mL of clobazam 10 mg/5 mL oral suspension are required to provide 4 weeks of treatment?
explanation: Single dose mg = 0.25 mg/kg × 17 kg = 4.25 mg. Single dose volume = (4.25 ÷ 10) × 5 = 2.125 mL → rounded to 2.0 mL. Daily volume = 2.0 × 2 = 4.0 mL. 4 weeks (28 days) = 4.0 × 28 = 112 mL.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: An 11-year-old girl weighing 36 kg requires rifampicin to prevent a secondary case of meningococcal meningitis. The BNFC dose is 10 mg/kg twice daily for 2 days. Rifampicin 100 mg/5 mL oral suspension is available. How many mL of rifampicin oral suspension are required to complete this prophylactic course?
explanation: Single dose = 10 mg/kg × 36 kg = 360 mg. Single dose volume = (360 ÷ 100) × 5 = 18 mL. Total doses = 2 BD × 2 days = 4 doses. Total volume = 4 × 18 = 72 mL.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A patient requests a salbutamol 100 micrograms/dose inhaler (200 doses per inhaler) for use as 2 puffs four times a day when required. Assuming maximum daily use, how many inhalers are needed to provide a 28-day supply?
explanation: Max daily use = 2 puffs × 4 doses = 8 puffs/day. 28-day total = 8 × 28 = 224 puffs. Inhalers needed = ceiling(224 ÷ 200) = 2 inhalers.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 9-year-old boy weighing 32 kg is prescribed amoxicillin 250 mg three times a day for 7 days for a chest infection. Amoxicillin 250 mg/5 mL oral suspension is supplied in 100 mL bottles. How many bottles should be dispensed?
explanation: Single dose volume = 5 mL. Total doses = 3 × 7 = 21. Total volume needed = 21 × 5 = 105 mL. Bottles needed = ceiling(105 ÷ 100) = 2 bottles.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A patient with type 1 diabetes uses 24 units of long-acting insulin once daily and 8 units of rapid-acting insulin three times daily with meals. Both insulins come in 3 mL pens (100 units/mL = 300 units per pen) supplied in packs of 5 pens. How many packs (of either type) are required to provide 28 days of supply, assuming separate pens for each insulin? (Express as combined total packs.)
explanation: Long-acting: 24 × 28 = 672 units → 3 pens (900 units) needed → 1 pack of 5. Rapid: 8 × 3 × 28 = 672 units → 3 pens needed → 1 pack of 5. Total = 2 packs (one of each insulin).

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A patient is starting nicotine replacement therapy with the following regimen: 21 mg patches for 6 weeks, then 14 mg patches for 2 weeks, then 7 mg patches for 2 weeks. One patch is applied daily. Each patch strength is supplied in packs of 7 patches. How many packs of patches in total are required for the full course?
explanation: 21 mg: 6 weeks × 7 days = 42 patches → 6 packs. 14 mg: 2 × 7 = 14 patches → 2 packs. 7 mg: 2 × 7 = 14 patches → 2 packs. Total = 6 + 2 + 2 = 10 packs.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A patient with bilateral open-angle glaucoma is prescribed latanoprost 50 micrograms/mL eye drops, 1 drop into each eye every evening. The drops are supplied in 2.5 mL bottles, with each bottle delivering approximately 50 drops. How many bottles are required for a 28-day supply?
explanation: Drops per day = 2 (one in each eye). Total drops over 28 days = 56. Bottles needed = ceiling(56 ÷ 50) = 2 bottles.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 50-year-old woman is prescribed sequential combined HRT: estradiol 2 mg one tablet daily continuously, plus medroxyprogesterone acetate 10 mg one tablet daily for the last 14 days of each 28-day cycle. How many medroxyprogesterone acetate 10 mg tablets are required for 1 year (13 cycles)?
explanation: Tablets per cycle = 14. Total = 14 × 13 cycles = 182 tablets. (Note: a year of HRT typically uses 13 × 28-day cycles = 364 days.)

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 72-year-old man with metastatic cancer is prescribed morphine sulfate 30 mg modified-release tablets, one tablet twice a day for 28 days. The tablets are supplied in packs of 28 tablets. How many packs are required for 28 days of treatment?
explanation: Daily tablets = 2. 28-day total = 2 × 28 = 56 tablets. Packs needed = 56 ÷ 28 = 2 packs.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 60-year-old patient with gastro-oesophageal reflux disease is prescribed lansoprazole 30 mg once daily for 4 weeks, then 15 mg once daily for a further 4 weeks. Both strengths are supplied in packs of 28 capsules. How many packs in total should be dispensed?
option_a: 1 pack of 30 mg, 1 pack of 15 mg
option_b: 2 packs of 30 mg, 1 pack of 15 mg
option_c: 2 packs of 30 mg, 2 packs of 15 mg
option_d: 3 packs of 30 mg, 1 pack of 15 mg
correct_answer: 1 pack of 30 mg, 1 pack of 15 mg
explanation: 30 mg phase: 28 days × 1 capsule = 28 capsules → 1 pack of 28. 15 mg phase: 28 days × 1 capsule = 28 capsules → 1 pack of 28.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A patient is prescribed a 21+7 (21 active pills then 7 placebo) combined oral contraceptive in a 28-day pack. She requests a 6-month supply (assume 6 months = 182 days). How many packs should be dispensed?
explanation: Days needed = 182. Packs needed = ceiling(182 ÷ 28) = ceiling(6.5) = 7 packs.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A patient with oral candidiasis is prescribed nystatin oral suspension 100,000 units four times a day for 14 days. The suspension is 100,000 units/mL and is supplied in 30 mL bottles. How many bottles should be dispensed?
explanation: Daily volume = 1 mL × 4 = 4 mL. 14-day total = 56 mL. Bottles = ceiling(56 ÷ 30) = 2 bottles.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A patient with severe eczema affecting most of the body uses an emollient cream at a rate of 200 g per week. The emollient is supplied in 500 g tubs. How many tubs are required for a 30-day supply? Round up to the nearest whole tub.
explanation: Daily use = 200 ÷ 7 = 28.57 g/day. 30-day total = 30 × 28.57 = 857.1 g. Tubs = ceiling(857.1 ÷ 500) = 2 tubs.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A patient with rheumatoid arthritis is prescribed methotrexate 17.5 mg PO once weekly. The pharmacy stocks methotrexate 2.5 mg tablets only. How many tablets are required for 12 weeks of treatment?
explanation: Tablets per dose = 17.5 ÷ 2.5 = 7 tablets. Weekly tablets = 7. Total over 12 weeks = 7 × 12 = 84 tablets. (Methotrexate dosing errors due to confusion with daily dosing have caused fatalities — this is an MHRA/CHM safety priority.)

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 45-year-old woman who cannot swallow tablets is prescribed co-amoxiclav 500/125 mg/5 mL oral suspension, 5 mL three times a day for 7 days. The suspension is supplied in 100 mL bottles. How many bottles should be dispensed?
explanation: Daily volume = 5 mL × 3 = 15 mL. 7-day total = 105 mL. Bottles = ceiling(105 ÷ 100) = 2 bottles.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A patient is prescribed H. pylori triple-therapy eradication for 7 days: amoxicillin 1 g twice daily, clarithromycin 500 mg twice daily, and omeprazole 20 mg twice daily. The pharmacy stocks amoxicillin 500 mg capsules. How many amoxicillin 500 mg capsules are required for the course?
explanation: Capsules per dose = 1000 ÷ 500 = 2. Daily capsules = 2 × 2 = 4. Total = 4 × 7 = 28 capsules.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A patient with stable angina is prescribed glyceryl trinitrate (GTN) sublingual spray, 1–2 sprays under the tongue when required. The spray contains 200 metered doses per canister. Assuming the patient uses an average of 2 sprays per day (1 spray per dose, twice a day), how many days will one canister last?
explanation: Daily use = 2 sprays. Days = 200 ÷ 2 = 100 days. (Note: GTN sprays should typically be replaced after 3 years even if doses remain, due to expiry.)

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 6-month-old girl weighing 7.2 kg is prescribed paracetamol oral suspension 120 mg/5 mL for post-immunisation pyrexia. The dose is 15 mg/kg every 6 hours when required, to a maximum of 4 doses in 24 hours. Each dose should be rounded to the nearest 0.1 mL. What is the maximum total volume, in mL, of paracetamol suspension this patient could receive in 24 hours?
explanation: Single dose = 15 × 7.2 = 108 mg. Volume = (108 ÷ 120) × 5 = 4.5 mL (no rounding needed). Max 24-hour volume = 4 × 4.5 = 18.0 mL.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 14 kg child requires a single stat dose of amoxicillin 50 mg/kg as endocarditis prophylaxis before a dental procedure. Amoxicillin 250 mg/5 mL oral suspension is available. What volume, in mL, should be administered?

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: An 11-year-old girl with vasculitis weighing 37 kg has a body surface area (BSA) of 1.2 m² and requires rituximab 750 mg/m². How many milligrams of rituximab are required for one dose?
explanation: Dose = 750 mg/m² × 1.2 m² = 900 mg.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 9 kg infant is prescribed cefalexin 30 mg/kg twice daily (max 1 g per dose). Cefalexin 250 mg/5 mL oral suspension is available. What volume, in mL, should be administered for each dose?
explanation: Dose per kg × weight = 30 × 9 = 270 mg per dose (below 1 g max). Volume = (270 ÷ 250) × 5 = 5.4 mL.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 45-year-old woman is 1.70 m tall and weighs 95 kg. The formula for body mass index is: BMI = weight (kg) ÷ height (m)². What is her BMI? Give your answer to one decimal place.
explanation: BMI = 95 ÷ (1.70 × 1.70) = 95 ÷ 2.89 = 32.87 ≈ 32.9 kg/m². (BMI ≥30 kg/m² indicates obesity.)

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A patient is prescribed flucloxacillin 500 mg four times a day for 7 days for cellulitis. What is the total dose, in grams, that the patient will receive over the entire course?
explanation: Total = 500 mg × 4 doses × 7 days = 14,000 mg = 14 g.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 78-year-old man with end-stage cancer is taking morphine sulfate immediate-release 30 mg PO every 12 hours. He becomes unable to swallow, and the team wishes to convert him to a continuous subcutaneous morphine sulfate infusion via a syringe driver over 24 hours. The PO to SC morphine conversion ratio is 2:1. How many mg of morphine sulfate should be loaded into the syringe driver for 24 hours?
explanation: Total daily PO morphine = 30 mg × 2 doses = 60 mg PO. SC equivalent = 60 ÷ 2 = 30 mg SC over 24 hours.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 65-year-old man with breast cancer is prescribed doxorubicin 60 mg/m² IV. His body surface area is 1.85 m². How many mg of doxorubicin should be administered? Give your answer to the nearest whole number.
explanation: Dose = 60 mg/m² × 1.85 m² = 111 mg.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A patient with heart failure is currently taking bisoprolol 1.25 mg once daily. The plan is to titrate the dose up to 5 mg once daily, increasing by 1.25 mg increments at weekly intervals. After how many weeks of titration will the patient reach the target dose of 5 mg once daily?
explanation: Sequence: Week 0 (current): 1.25 mg. Week 1: 2.5 mg. Week 2: 3.75 mg. Week 3: 5 mg. After 3 weeks of titration, the target dose is reached.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A patient is taking codeine phosphate 60 mg four times a day for chronic pain. The plan is to convert her to oral morphine sulfate. The PO codeine to PO morphine conversion ratio is 10:1. What is the equivalent total daily dose of oral morphine, in mg?
explanation: Total daily codeine = 60 × 4 = 240 mg. Equivalent oral morphine = 240 ÷ 10 = 24 mg/day.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: An 80 kg man with sepsis is prescribed a vancomycin loading dose of 25 mg/kg IV. The pharmacy supplies vancomycin 500 mg vials. How many vials are required to provide the loading dose?
explanation: Loading dose = 25 × 80 = 2000 mg. Vials needed = ceiling(2000 ÷ 500) = 4 vials.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 6-year-old child with seasonal allergic rhinitis is prescribed cetirizine 5 mg once daily for 14 days. What is the total cumulative dose, in mg, the child will receive over the full course?
explanation: Total dose = 5 mg × 14 days = 70 mg.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 73 kg patient is prescribed azathioprine 2 mg/kg PO once daily. Azathioprine is supplied as 25 mg and 50 mg tablets only. What is the most practical daily dose, rounding to the nearest 25 mg increment to use whole tablets?
explanation: Calculated dose = 2 × 73 = 146 mg. Nearest 25 mg increment = 150 mg, achievable with 3 × 50 mg tablets. (Tablets should not be split unless scored and licensed for division.)

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A patient on a methadone induction regimen receives an initial 10 mg dose at 09:00, with 5 mg additional doses permitted every 4 hours up to a 24-hour maximum of 30 mg. What is the maximum number of 5 mg additional doses the patient can receive in the first 24 hours after the initial dose?
explanation: Initial: 10 mg. Maximum total in 24 h: 30 mg. Available for additional doses: 30 − 10 = 20 mg. Number of 5 mg doses = 20 ÷ 5 = 4 additional doses.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A patient with iron-deficiency anaemia is prescribed ferrous sulfate 200 mg one tablet three times a day. Each 200 mg ferrous sulfate tablet contains 65 mg of elemental iron. What is the total daily dose of elemental iron, in mg?
explanation: Daily elemental iron = 65 mg × 3 doses = 195 mg.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: An 85 kg patient with confirmed deep vein thrombosis is prescribed enoxaparin 1.5 mg/kg subcutaneously once daily. What is the calculated dose, in mg? Give your answer to one decimal place.
explanation: Dose = 1.5 mg/kg × 85 kg = 127.5 mg. (In practice, the dose is rounded to the nearest available pre-filled syringe size.)

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A patient with breast cancer has previously received a cumulative dose of 600 mg of doxorubicin. She has a body surface area of 1.7 m² and the lifetime maximum cumulative dose of doxorubicin is 450 mg/m² to limit cardiotoxicity. The planned regimen is 50 mg/m² per cycle. How many additional full cycles can she receive without exceeding the lifetime maximum?
explanation: Lifetime max = 450 × 1.7 = 765 mg. Already received = 600 mg. Remaining allowance = 165 mg. Per cycle = 50 × 1.7 = 85 mg. Full cycles possible = floor(165 ÷ 85) = 1 cycle.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A patient was taking warfarin 5 mg once daily but had warfarin held for 2 days due to an elevated INR of 4.5. On day 3, warfarin is restarted at 50% of the previous dose. What is the new total weekly dose, in mg, assuming no further dose changes are made over a 7-day week?
explanation: New daily dose = 50% × 5 mg = 2.5 mg. Weekly dose = 2.5 × 7 = 17.5 mg.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: An 8-year-old boy with acute otitis media is prescribed amoxicillin 500 mg three times a day for 5 days. Amoxicillin 250 mg/5 mL oral suspension is available. What is the total volume, in mL, of suspension required for the full course?
explanation: Single dose volume = (500 ÷ 250) × 5 = 10 mL. Daily volume = 10 × 3 = 30 mL. Course volume = 30 × 5 = 150 mL.

section_title: Calculation Topics for the GPhC Pre-Reg Exam
question_text: A 16-year-old patient weighing 45 kg requires IV paracetamol post-operatively. In adults weighing under 50 kg, the dose is 15 mg/kg per dose to a maximum of 60 mg/kg per 24 hours. What is the maximum number of doses of 15 mg/kg this patient can receive in 24 hours?
explanation: Single dose = 15 × 45 = 675 mg. Max 24-h dose = 60 × 45 = 2700 mg. Max doses = 2700 ÷ 675 = 4 doses (typically given every 6 hours).
